instruction: What are the key provisions of this bill?
input:
Bill Number: HB 3333
Title: Comprehensive Immigration Reform Act
Description: Creates a pathway to citizenship for undocumented immigrants who have lived in the US for 10+ years, strengthens border security with technology investments, reforms the visa system to prioritize skills-based immigration, and increases funding for immigration courts to reduce case backlogs.
Status: In Committee
Committee: Committee on the Judiciary
Last Action: Subcommittee hearing scheduled
output: The key provisions of HB 3333 include: (1) A pathway to citizenship for undocumented immigrants with 10+ years of US residency, (2) Enhanced border security through technology investments, (3) Visa system reform prioritizing skills-based immigration, and (4) Increased funding for immigration courts to reduce backlogs. The bill takes a comprehensive approach addressing both enforcement and reform aspects of immigration policy.

instruction: Answer the question using only the provided legislative context.
input:
Question: What type of energy sources does the bill promote?
Context: Bill Number: HB 2580
Title: Next Generation Nuclear Energy Act
Description: Provides $6 billion for advanced nuclear reactor research and development, streamlines NRC licensing for small modular reactors, creates a nuclear energy workforce training program, and establishes safety standards for next-generation reactor designs including molten salt and high-temperature gas reactors.
Status: Passed House
Committee: Committee on Energy and Commerce
Last Action: Passed House 310-115, referred to Senate
output: Based on the provided context, HB 2580 promotes nuclear energy sources, specifically advanced nuclear reactor technologies. The bill supports next-generation designs including small modular reactors, molten salt reactors, and high-temperature gas reactors through $6 billion in R&D funding, streamlined licensing, and new safety standards.

instruction: Analyze the fiscal implications of this bill.
input:
Bill Number: HB 4400
Title: National Childcare and Early Learning Act
Description: Establishes a federal childcare subsidy program capping family childcare costs at 7% of household income. Allocates $40 billion annually for the program. Raises childcare worker minimum pay to $15/hour with annual adjustments. Provides construction grants for new childcare facilities in underserved areas.
Status: Introduced
Committee: Committee on Education and the Workforce
Last Action: CBO score requested
output: HB 4400 carries substantial fiscal implications with $40 billion in annual federal spending. Key cost drivers include: (1) Subsidies bridging the gap between 7% of family income and actual childcare costs, (2) Wage floor increases for childcare workers, and (3) Capital grants for facility construction. The CBO score request indicates Congress is evaluating the budgetary impact. Potential offsets could include increased tax revenue from higher workforce participation rates among parents, though net costs would likely remain significant. The $40B annual price tag makes this one of the larger social spending proposals under consideration.

instruction: What is the current status of this bill?
input:
Bill Number: SB 3456
Title: Digital Privacy and Data Protection Act
Description: Establishes federal standards for consumer data privacy, requiring companies to obtain explicit consent before collecting personal data. Creates a new Bureau of Digital Privacy within the FTC and imposes penalties for data breaches affecting more than 10,000 users.
Status: Passed Committee
Committee: Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation
Last Action: Reported favorably by committee, placed on Senate calendar
output: The Digital Privacy and Data Protection Act (SB 3456) has passed committee review. It was reported favorably by the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation and has been placed on the Senate legislative calendar for floor consideration.

instruction: Given the following bill details, draft three factual questions that could be answered using only the provided context.
input:
Bill Number: SB 4820
Title: Rural Broadband Expansion and Digital Equity Act
Description: Provides $12 billion in grants to internet service providers for expanding broadband to unserved rural areas. Defines minimum broadband speed as 100/20 Mbps. Creates a digital navigator program to help rural residents adopt internet services. Requires quarterly progress reports from grant recipients.
Status: Passed Senate
Committee: Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation
Last Action: Passed Senate 72-25, referred to House Energy and Commerce Committee
output:
1. What is the minimum broadband speed defined by the Rural Broadband Expansion and Digital Equity Act? (Answer: 100/20 Mbps)
2. How much federal funding does SB 4820 provide for rural broadband expansion? (Answer: $12 billion in grants)
3. What reporting requirements does the bill impose on grant recipients? (Answer: Quarterly progress reports)

instruction: Answer the question using only the provided legislative context.
input:
Question: What workforce development provisions are included in this bill?
Context: Bill Number: SB 6000
Title: Advanced Manufacturing Workforce Act
Description: Creates apprenticeship programs in advanced manufacturing partnering with community colleges and industry. Provides $3 billion for manufacturing training centers, offers tax credits for companies hiring apprentices, and establishes a Manufacturing Skills Certification program recognized across states.
Status: Passed Committee
Committee: Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions
Last Action: Reported favorably by committee
output: The workforce development provisions in SB 6000 include: (1) Apprenticeship programs partnering community colleges with industry for hands-on manufacturing training, (2) $3 billion in funding for dedicated manufacturing training centers, (3) Tax credit incentives for companies that hire apprentices, and (4) A nationally recognized Manufacturing Skills Certification program with cross-state portability, ensuring workers' credentials are valued regardless of location.

instruction: Analyze the legislative history and trajectory of this bill.
input:
Bill Number: HB 2468
Title: Small Business Innovation and Growth Act
Description: Doubles the Small Business Innovation Research (SBIR) program funding, creates tax incentives for angel investors in startups, streamlines SBA loan processes, and establishes regional innovation hubs.
Status: Passed House
Committee: Committee on Small Business
Last Action: Passed House 285-140, referred to Senate Small Business Committee
output: HB 2468 shows strong legislative momentum. Passing the House with a 285-140 vote indicates substantial bipartisan support (a margin of 145 votes). The bill has moved from introduction through committee consideration to a successful floor vote. Its referral to the Senate Small Business Committee is the standard next step. The strong House vote margin suggests it has a reasonable path forward in the Senate, though Senate procedures and potential amendments could alter its timeline.

instruction: Determine whether the provided context contains enough evidence to answer the question. If not, state that evidence is insufficient.
input:
Question: How many co-sponsors does the veterans healthcare bill have?
Context: Bill Number: HB 4000
Title: Veterans Healthcare Expansion Act
Description: Expands VA healthcare eligibility to include all combat veterans regardless of service period, increases VA hospital funding by $8 billion annually, and creates mobile health units for rural veteran populations.
Status: Introduced
Committee: Committee on Veterans Affairs
Last Action: Referred to committee
output: Not enough evidence in the retrieved context. The bill description covers eligibility expansion, funding amounts, and mobile health units, but co-sponsor information is not included in the available data. Co-sponsor counts would need to be retrieved from the congressional record or bill tracking system.

instruction: Classify this bill by policy area and explain the classification.
input:
Bill Number: SB 3690
Title: Water Infrastructure and Drought Resilience Act
Description: Authorizes $15 billion for water infrastructure repairs, establishes a national water recycling program, funds desalination research, and creates drought contingency plans for western states. Includes provisions for tribal water rights settlements.
Status: Introduced
Committee: Committee on Environment and Public Works
Last Action: Referred to committee
output: Primary policy area: Environment and Natural Resources. Secondary areas: Infrastructure, Tribal Affairs. This classification is based on the bill's focus on water systems (environmental infrastructure), drought management (natural resource conservation), and tribal water rights (indigenous affairs). The committee assignment to Environment and Public Works confirms the primary classification.

instruction: Explain the potential impact of this bill if enacted.
input:
Bill Number: HB 4567
Title: Universal Pre-K Education Act
Description: Establishes a federally funded universal pre-kindergarten program for all children ages 3-4. Provides $10 billion annually to states for program implementation, sets quality standards for pre-K providers, and includes provisions for teacher salary parity with K-12 educators.
Status: Introduced
Committee: Committee on Education and the Workforce
Last Action: Referred to committee
output: If enacted, HB 4567 would significantly expand early childhood education access nationwide. The $10 billion annual federal investment would enable states to offer free pre-K to all 3-4 year-olds, potentially affecting millions of families. Teacher salary parity provisions could attract more qualified educators to early childhood programs. Research suggests universal pre-K can reduce achievement gaps, improve long-term educational outcomes, and allow more parents to participate in the workforce.

instruction: Explain the potential impact of this bill if enacted.
input:
Bill Number: SB 2750
Title: National Paid Family Leave Act
Description: Establishes a federal paid family and medical leave program providing 12 weeks of partial wage replacement (up to 66% of wages, capped at $1,200/week) for new parents, family caregivers, and workers with serious health conditions. Funded through a 0.4% payroll tax split between employers and employees.
Status: In Committee
Committee: Committee on Finance
Last Action: CBO score released, awaiting markup
output: If enacted, SB 2750 would create the first federal paid family leave program in US history. Impact areas: (1) Workers would gain financial security during major life events with 12 weeks at 66% wage replacement, (2) The 0.4% payroll tax (0.2% each for employer/employee) would cost roughly $40/year for a $50K earner, (3) Employers would see reduced turnover as studies show paid leave improves retention, (4) The economy could benefit from increased labor force participation, particularly among women, (5) Small businesses may face challenges managing extended employee absences despite the benefit. The CBO score release suggests fiscal analysis is complete, moving the bill toward potential markup.

instruction: Answer the question using only the provided legislative context.
input:
Question: How does this bill address prescription drug costs?
Context: Bill Number: SB 7700
Title: Prescription Drug Affordability Act
Description: Allows Medicare to negotiate prices for the 250 most expensive drugs, caps annual out-of-pocket drug spending at $2,000 for Medicare beneficiaries, requires drug companies to pay rebates if prices rise faster than inflation, and imports Canadian-approved prescription drugs.
Status: In Committee
Committee: Committee on Finance
Last Action: Committee hearing completed
output: SB 7700 addresses prescription drug costs through four mechanisms: (1) Medicare negotiation for the 250 most expensive drugs, (2) a $2,000 annual out-of-pocket spending cap for Medicare beneficiaries, (3) mandatory manufacturer rebates when prices exceed inflation, and (4) importation of lower-cost Canadian-approved drugs.

instruction: Analyze the legislative history and trajectory of this bill.
input:
Bill Number: SB 1500
Title: Bipartisan Infrastructure Investment Act
Description: Authorizes $1.2 trillion for infrastructure over 8 years covering roads, bridges, rail, broadband, water, and electric grid improvements.
Status: Signed into Law
Committee: Multiple committees (Commerce, Environment and Public Works, Banking)
Last Action: Signed by the President after passage by both chambers with bipartisan support
output: SB 1500 completed the full legislative journey from introduction to enactment. Notable trajectory features: (1) The bill was considered by multiple committees reflecting its broad scope, (2) It achieved bipartisan support in both chambers, which is increasingly rare for legislation of this magnitude, (3) The $1.2 trillion authorization over 8 years represents one of the largest infrastructure investments in recent history. The bipartisan nature of its passage suggests it addressed priorities shared across party lines, likely through compromise on spending levels and project selection criteria.

instruction: Answer the question using only the provided legislative context.
input:
Question: What environmental protections does the mining reform bill include?
Context: Bill Number: HB 3300
Title: Hardrock Mining Reform and Reclamation Act
Description: Updates the 1872 Mining Law to require royalty payments on minerals extracted from federal lands at 8% for new operations and 4% for existing mines. Mandates environmental impact assessments before new mining permits, requires companies to post reclamation bonds, and establishes a fund for cleaning up abandoned mine sites using royalty revenue.
Status: Passed House
Committee: Committee on Natural Resources
Last Action: Passed House 240-185, referred to Senate
output: The bill includes several environmental protections: (1) Mandatory environmental impact assessments before issuing new mining permits, (2) Required reclamation bonds ensuring companies fund site restoration, and (3) An abandoned mine cleanup fund financed by royalty revenue. These provisions ensure that mining operations on federal lands must assess and mitigate environmental damage, and that financial mechanisms exist for both active site reclamation and legacy site cleanup.

instruction: Compare the following two bills and highlight their similarities and differences.
input:
Bill A - HB 7000: Secure Border Infrastructure Act. Allocates $18 billion for physical border barriers, surveillance technology, and additional Border Patrol agents. Status: Passed House.

Bill B - SB 8000: Comprehensive Border Security and Immigration Reform Act. Provides $10 billion for border technology (drones, sensors, cameras), creates new legal immigration pathways, and reforms the asylum process. Status: In Committee.
output: Both bills address border security but differ significantly in scope and approach. HB 7000 focuses primarily on enforcement through physical barriers, surveillance, and personnel ($18B), while SB 8000 takes a broader approach combining technology-based security ($10B) with immigration reform including new legal pathways and asylum process changes. HB 7000 is purely an enforcement bill; SB 8000 combines enforcement with policy reform. HB 7000 has advanced further (passed House) compared to SB 8000 (still in committee), suggesting the enforcement-only approach has stronger legislative support currently.

instruction: Summarize the following legislative bill.
input:
Bill Number: HB 6200
Title: National Apprenticeship Act Reauthorization
Description: Reauthorizes and modernizes the national apprenticeship system. Expands registered apprenticeships to new industries including technology, healthcare, and clean energy. Provides $3.5 billion over 5 years for apprenticeship grants. Includes a youth apprenticeship program for ages 16-24 and creates industry-recognized apprenticeship programs with quality standards.
Status: Passed House
Committee: Committee on Education and the Workforce
Last Action: Passed House 247-188
output: The National Apprenticeship Act Reauthorization (HB 6200) modernizes the apprenticeship system by expanding into tech, healthcare, and clean energy sectors. With $3.5 billion over 5 years, it funds registered apprenticeship grants, introduces a youth program for ages 16-24, and establishes quality standards for industry-recognized programs. The bill passed the House 247-188 and moves to Senate consideration.

instruction: What is the current status of this bill?
input:
Bill Number: HB 7890
Title: National Infrastructure Modernization Act
Description: Authorizes $500 billion in federal spending over 10 years for infrastructure projects including bridges, roads, public transit, broadband expansion, and water systems. Establishes a competitive grant program for state and local governments.
Status: Signed into Law
Committee: Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure
Last Action: Signed by the President
output: The National Infrastructure Modernization Act (HB 7890) has been signed into law by the President. This bill authorizes $500 billion over 10 years for infrastructure projects covering bridges, roads, transit, broadband, and water systems through competitive grants to state and local governments.

Committee: Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation
Last Action: Referred to committee
output: Not enough evidence in the retrieved context. A CBO score or the full bill text would be needed to answer this question.

instruction: What are the key provisions of this bill?
input:
Bill Number: SB 8888
Title: Cybersecurity Enhancement and Critical Infrastructure Protection Act
Description: Mandates cybersecurity standards for critical infrastructure operators, creates a federal cyber incident reporting system with 72-hour notification requirements, establishes a Cybersecurity Reserve Corps for emergency response, and provides grants for state and local cybersecurity improvements.
Status: Passed Senate
Committee: Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs
Last Action: Passed Senate, referred to House committee
output: Key provisions of SB 8888 include: (1) Mandatory cybersecurity standards for critical infrastructure, (2) A federal cyber incident reporting system requiring notification within 72 hours, (3) Creation of a Cybersecurity Reserve Corps for rapid emergency response, and (4) Grant programs for state and local cybersecurity upgrades. The bill has already passed the Senate and moves to House consideration.

instruction: Summarize the following legislative bill.
input:
Bill Number: SB 6543
Title: Election Security and Modernization Act
Description: Requires all voting systems to produce paper ballot records, establishes federal cybersecurity standards for election infrastructure, creates a $2 billion grant program for states to upgrade voting equipment, mandates post-election audits in federal races, and prohibits internet connectivity for vote tabulation systems.
Status: Passed Committee
Committee: Committee on Rules and Administration
Last Action: Reported favorably, placed on Senate calendar
output: The Election Security and Modernization Act (SB 6543) strengthens election infrastructure through five key provisions: paper ballot requirements for all voting systems, federal cybersecurity standards, $2 billion in state grants for equipment upgrades, mandatory post-election audits, and a ban on internet-connected tabulation systems. The bill has cleared committee and awaits Senate floor consideration.

instruction: Explain the legislative process stage this bill is currently in.
input:
Bill Number: HB 9500
Title: Federal Data Center Consolidation Act
Description: Requires federal agencies to consolidate redundant data centers, transition to cloud computing where feasible, and report annual savings.
Status: Engrossed
Committee: Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
Last Action: Passed House, engrossed and sent to Senate
output: The bill is in the 'engrossed' stage, meaning the House has passed it and the official final text has been prepared and transmitted to the Senate. This is a procedural step between House passage and Senate consideration. The Senate will receive the bill, assign it to the appropriate committee, and begin its own review process. The bill must pass the Senate (potentially with amendments) before it can advance to the President.

instruction: Summarize the following legislative bill.
input:
Bill Number: HB 1234
Title: Autonomous Vehicle Safety and Regulation Act
Description: This bill establishes a comprehensive regulatory framework for the testing and deployment of autonomous vehicles on public highways. It mandates safety certification requirements, liability frameworks for accidents involving self-driving cars, and creates an oversight committee within the Department of Transportation.
Status: Introduced
Committee: Transportation and Infrastructure
Last Action: Referred to subcommittee on highways and transit
output: The Autonomous Vehicle Safety and Regulation Act (HB 1234) creates a regulatory framework for self-driving vehicles on public roads. Key provisions include mandatory safety certifications, accident liability rules, and a new DOT oversight committee. The bill has been introduced and referred to the Transportation subcommittee on highways and transit.

instruction: What is the current status of this bill?
input:
Bill Number: SB 4100
Title: Comprehensive Tax Reform and Simplification Act
Description: Simplifies the individual tax code by reducing brackets from 7 to 3, increases the standard deduction, eliminates certain itemized deductions, lowers corporate tax rate to 21%, and creates a pass-through business income deduction.
Status: Vetoed
Committee: Committee on Finance
Last Action: President vetoed the bill, override vote pending in Senate
output: The Comprehensive Tax Reform and Simplification Act (SB 4100) has been vetoed by the President. The bill successfully passed through the entire legislative process—committee, floor votes in both chambers—but was rejected at the executive level. The next step is a potential veto override attempt in the Senate, which would require a two-thirds supermajority vote to succeed. If the Senate override fails, the bill dies unless reintroduced in a future session.

input:
Bill Number: HB 8642
Title: Affordable Housing and Community Development Act
Description: Invests $25 billion in affordable housing construction, expands the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC), provides down payment assistance for first-time homebuyers earning below 80% of area median income, and creates a Community Land Trust grant program.

Committee: Committee on Financial Services

instruction: Identify key amendments that could strengthen this bill.
input:
Bill Number: SB 7250
Title: Clean Water Act Modernization
Description: Updates water quality standards for PFAS and emerging contaminants, requires public water systems to test for 50 additional chemicals, provides $8 billion for water treatment plant upgrades, and establishes a contamination notification system for affected communities.
Status: In Committee
Committee: Committee on Environment and Public Works
Last Action: Public comment period opened
output: Potential strengthening amendments: (1) Add provisions for private well testing since the bill only covers public water systems, leaving millions of private well users without protections, (2) Include funding for lead service line replacement as a related water safety priority, (3) Add environmental justice provisions ensuring disadvantaged communities receive prioritized treatment plant upgrades, (4) Establish a revolving loan fund rather than one-time grants for sustainable long-term infrastructure investment, (5) Include agricultural runoff regulation as a major source of water contamination not addressed in the current text.

output: Not enough evidence in the retrieved context. While the bill describes $20 billion in green infrastructure investment, the description does not include job creation estimates or economic impact projections. instruction: Given a legislative query, determine if the question can be answered from the bills database or if it requires external information.
input: Question: Which bills related to climate change were introduced in the current session?
output: This question can likely be answered from the bills database. By searching bill titles and descriptions for keywords related to climate change (e.g., 'climate', 'emissions', 'carbon', 'greenhouse gas', 'renewable energy'), and filtering by the current legislative session, the database should return relevant results with bill numbers, titles, descriptions, and current status.

instruction: Compare the following two bills and highlight their similarities and differences.
input:
Bill A - SB 6600: Universal Basic Income Pilot Act. Creates a 3-year federal UBI pilot in 10 cities providing $1,000/month to 10,000 participants per city. Cost: $3.6 billion. Status: Introduced.

Bill B - HB 7700: Earned Income Tax Credit Expansion Act. Doubles the EITC for childless workers, expands age eligibility to 19-67, and increases phase-out thresholds. Cost: $12 billion/year. Status: Passed Committee.
output: Both bills aim to reduce poverty and boost income for lower-income Americans, but through fundamentally different mechanisms. SB 6600 tests unconditional universal payments ($1K/month) in a limited pilot, while HB 7700 expands an existing conditional tax credit tied to employment. Key differences: (1) Scale: SB 6600 is a $3.6B pilot for 100K people vs. HB 7700's $12B/year affecting millions, (2) Conditionality: UBI has no work requirement; EITC requires earned income, (3) Political feasibility: HB 7700 has advanced further (passed committee) as it builds on proven policy, while UBI remains experimental and more politically contentious, (4) Philosophy: UBI assumes income should be a right; EITC incentivizes work participation.

instruction: Classify this bill by policy area and explain the classification.
input:
Bill Number: SB 5050
Title: National Mental Health Crisis Response Act
Description: Establishes a national 988 crisis response infrastructure with mobile crisis teams in every state. Provides $5 billion for mental health facility expansion, requires insurance parity enforcement for mental health coverage, creates a mental health professional loan forgiveness program, and funds research into early intervention programs.
Status: Passed Senate
Committee: Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions
Last Action: Passed Senate 88-10, referred to House
output: Primary policy area: Healthcare and Public Health. Secondary areas: Education (workforce training), Finance (insurance parity). The bill is fundamentally a healthcare measure focused on mental health crisis infrastructure, treatment facilities, and insurance coverage. The loan forgiveness component touches education policy, and insurance parity requirements involve financial regulation. The near-unanimous Senate vote (88-10) suggests strong bipartisan support, typical for mental health legislation.

instruction: Identify potential stakeholders who would be most affected by this bill.
input:
Bill Number: HB 7531
Title: Gig Economy Worker Protection Act
Description: Establishes minimum earnings guarantees for gig economy workers, requires platform companies to provide portable benefits including health insurance contributions, creates a dispute resolution process for deactivated workers, and mandates transparency in algorithmic pay calculations.
Status: In Committee
Committee: Committee on Education and the Workforce
Last Action: Hearing scheduled with testimony from ride-share drivers and tech company representatives
output: Key stakeholders affected: (1) Gig workers (ride-share drivers, delivery workers, freelancers) who would gain earnings protections and benefits, (2) Platform companies (Uber, Lyft, DoorDash, etc.) facing new cost obligations, (3) Consumers who may see price increases from higher platform operating costs, (4) Health insurance providers who would administer portable benefits, (5) State labor agencies that may need to align existing regulations, and (6) Traditional employers competing with gig platforms for workers.

instruction: Assess whether this bill is likely to advance and why.
input:
Bill Number: HB 6120
Title: Federal Term Limits Constitutional Amendment
Description: Proposes a constitutional amendment limiting members of the House to 6 terms (12 years) and Senators to 2 terms (12 years). Includes a grandfather clause for current members and would take effect after ratification by 38 states.
Status: Introduced
Committee: Committee on the Judiciary
Last Action: Referred to subcommittee on the Constitution
output: This bill faces significant challenges in advancing. Constitutional amendments require a two-thirds vote in both chambers and ratification by 38 states, making them exceptionally difficult to pass. Term limits proposals have historically lacked sufficient support from incumbent legislators who would be directly affected. The bill is still at the earliest stage (subcommittee referral) and similar past proposals have not advanced beyond committee. While term limits poll well with the public, the structural barriers to constitutional amendments make enactment unlikely in the near term.

instruction: Explain the potential impact of this bill if enacted.
input:
Bill Number: SB 9999
Title: Federal Minimum Wage Increase Act
Description: Gradually raises the federal minimum wage from $7.25 to $15.00 per hour over four years. Includes automatic annual adjustments tied to the Consumer Price Index after reaching $15. Provides tax credits for small businesses during the transition period.
Status: In Committee
Committee: Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions
Last Action: Hearing completed, awaiting markup
output: Enacting SB 9999 would double the federal minimum wage, directly affecting an estimated 30+ million workers. The phased approach over four years gives businesses time to adjust, while CPI-linked automatic increases would prevent wage stagnation. Small business tax credits mitigate transition costs. Potential impacts include increased consumer spending power, reduced poverty rates, and possible price increases in labor-intensive industries. The gradual rollout is designed to balance worker benefits against business adaptation needs.

instruction: Assess whether this bill is likely to advance and why.
input:
Bill Number: HB 1600
Title: Social Media Age Verification and Child Safety Act
Description: Requires social media platforms to verify user age, prohibits accounts for children under 13, mandates parental consent for users 13-16, limits algorithmic content promotion to minors, and imposes fines of $50,000 per violation on platforms.
Status: Passed House
Committee: Committee on Energy and Commerce
Last Action: Passed House 395-30, bipartisan support
output: This bill has very strong prospects for advancement. Evidence: (1) The 395-30 House vote shows overwhelming bipartisan support (93%), which is exceptionally rare, (2) Child online safety has emerged as one of the few genuinely bipartisan issues in current politics, (3) Similar state-level legislation has already passed in multiple states, demonstrating broad public support, (4) The bill's provisions align with growing public concern about social media's impact on youth. The main challenge in the Senate would be potential industry lobbying and First Amendment concerns, but the massive House margin suggests these obstacles have been largely addressed.

instruction: Identify potential stakeholders who would be most affected by this bill.
input:
Bill Number: SB 2900
Title: Federal Student Loan Reform Act
Description: Caps federal student loan interest rates at 3%, creates an income-driven repayment plan capping payments at 5% of discretionary income, forgives remaining balances after 20 years of payments, eliminates origination fees, and allows refinancing of existing federal loans at current rates.
Status: In Committee
Committee: Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions
Last Action: Hearing held with testimony from student advocacy groups
output: Key stakeholders: (1) Current student loan borrowers (43+ million Americans) who would benefit from lower rates and income-driven repayment, (2) Prospective college students facing reduced borrowing costs, (3) The federal government as the primary lender bearing reduced interest revenue, (4) Loan servicers whose business models would change, (5) Higher education institutions that may see enrollment increases from reduced financial barriers, (6) Taxpayers who ultimately bear the cost of loan forgiveness provisions, and (7) Private student lenders who may face competitive pressure from improved federal terms.

instruction: Answer the question using only the provided legislative context.
input:
Question: What tax provisions are included for small businesses?
Context: Bill Number: HB 8100
Title: Small Business Tax Relief and Simplification Act
Description: Increases the Section 179 expensing limit to $2 million, creates a simplified quarterly tax filing option for businesses under $1 million revenue, provides a 10% tax credit for hiring from disadvantaged communities, doubles the startup cost deduction to $10,000, and establishes a 3-year tax holiday for businesses in designated Opportunity Zones.
Status: In Committee
Committee: Committee on Ways and Means
Last Action: Hearing held
output: HB 8100 includes five small business tax provisions: (1) Increased Section 179 expensing limit to $2 million for equipment and asset purchases, (2) Simplified quarterly filing for businesses under $1M revenue, (3) 10% tax credit for hiring from disadvantaged communities, (4) Doubled startup cost deduction to $10,000, and (5) A 3-year tax holiday for businesses in Opportunity Zones. Together, these provisions aim to reduce both tax burden and compliance complexity for small businesses.

instruction: Determine whether the provided context contains enough evidence to answer the question. If not, state that evidence is insufficient.
input:
Question: What is the timeline for implementing the AI regulation bill's provisions?
Context: Bill Number: HB 9876
Title: Artificial Intelligence Accountability Act
Description: Requires AI systems used in hiring, lending, and criminal justice to undergo bias audits. Companies must disclose when AI is used in decision-making processes.
Status: Introduced
Committee: Committee on Science, Space, and Technology
output: Not enough evidence in the retrieved context. The bill description mentions bias audit requirements and disclosure mandates but does not specify an implementation timeline. Additional bill text or committee reports would be needed to determine the phased rollout schedule.
